draft.introduction: Esther 4 contains Mordecai’s plea for Esther to intervene with the king to save the Jewish people from Haman’s genocidal decree. His words—“who knows whether you have come to the kingdom for such a time as this?”—frame providence as operating through human courage. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And Mordecai said to convey to Esther: “Do not think to yourself that in the king’s house you will escape any more than all the other Jews. For if you keep silent at this time, relief and deliverance will rise for the Jews from another place, but you and your father’s house will perish. And who knows whether you have come to the kingdom for such a time as this?”

Then Esther told them to reply to Mordecai: “Go, gather all the Jews to be found in Susa, and hold a fast on my behalf, and do not eat or drink for three days, night or day. I and my young women will also fast in the same way. Then I will go to the king, though it is against the law, and if I perish, I perish.”

translator_notes.note: Mordecai’s confidence that deliverance will arise “from another place” even if Esther fails is the closest the Hebrew Esther comes to mentioning God (who is never named in the book). The Septuagint additions to Esther make the divine role explicit, but the Hebrew text leaves providence unnamed.
